Hi friends =F0=9F=91=8B,=20
Happy Tuesday!=20
Several articles on Not Boring this year have formed a semi-intentional ser=
ies.=20
In retrospect, there is a clear thread. Many of the companies I=E2=80=99m m=
ost excited about are doing the opposite of what tech companies have been t=
aught to do. They=E2=80=99re vertically integrating.=20
I=E2=80=99m splitting this essay into two parts. Part I today will set the =
foundation. It=E2=80=99s a rare Not Boring essay that fits within the email=
 length limit. Part II next week will dive into specific case studies of Ve=
rtical Integrators, the advantages they have, the challenges they face, and=
 some observations and predictions.=20
Let=E2=80=99s get to it.=20
Vertical Integrators: Part I
Out of all of the Deep Dives I=E2=80=99ve written on Techno-Industrials [ h=
ttps://substack.com/redirect/2906dc6f-3eec-400a-98ba-8be09dc7570c?j=3DeyJ1I=
joiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] over the past y=
ear, the one section=C2=A0 that stands out most clearly in my mind comes fr=
om the piece on Base Power Company [ https://substack.com/redirect/4054d256=
-0fa9-43f3-a64e-09aacbabf96e?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXX=
epKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ].
=E2=80=9CBase assembles and will soon manufacture battery packs itself, but=
 it doesn=E2=80=99t make battery cells=E2=80=A6 Base believes that cells wi=
ll be commoditized, and that pack assembly allows it to benefit from that c=
ommoditization and potentially integrate new cell types as better chemistri=
es emerge.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
It=E2=80=99s a concrete example of a trend I=E2=80=99ve noticed towards ver=
tical integration among companies building physical products.=C2=A0
Just as Aggregators [ https://substack.com/redirect/18320604-8b44-461e-ab3a=
-b5d84fa43afd?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8Ex=
kRNpU ] built some of the biggest companies in the Internet era, Vertical I=
ntegrators will be the biggest winners of the Techno-Industrial Revolution =
[ https://substack.com/redirect/2906dc6f-3eec-400a-98ba-8be09dc7570c?j=3Dey=
J1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ].=C2=A0=C2=A0=
=C2=A0
Vertical Integrators are companies that:
Integrate multiple cutting-edge-but-proven technologies.
Develop significant in-house capabilities across their stack.
Modularize commoditized components while controlling overall system integra=
tion.
Compete directly with incumbents.
Offer products that are better, faster, or cheaper (often all three).
For Vertical Integrators, the integration is the innovation.=C2=A0
Let=E2=80=99s go back to the Base example to unpack that. Battery pack asse=
mbly is one of the many activities the company does. As I wrote in the piec=
e:=C2=A0
To deploy batteries and fix the grid, Base can=E2=80=99t just build a batte=
ry; it needs to build a vertically integrated power company around batterie=
s.=C2=A0
The power company needs to excel at a whole lot of things: manufacturing, f=
inance, distributed systems, power trading, customer acquisition, consumer =
software, regulatory, and installation, to start. It needs to become a Reta=
il Electric Provider (REP) itself.
Each of those things is hard but achievable. What=E2=80=99s novel is their =
combination into one coherent product. I recommend reading the Deep Dive [ =
https://substack.com/redirect/4054d256-0fa9-43f3-a64e-09aacbabf96e?j=3DeyJ1=
IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] to understand =
how the company has thought about fitting them together. Each permutation c=
omes with trade-offs: =E2=80=9CMove one thing here, and something else chan=
ges there. Move that and something else shifts.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
The decision to manufacture packs but not cells is one of many such decisio=
ns, and it highlights the difference between =E2=80=9Cdeep tech=E2=80=9D co=
mpanies and Vertical Integrators. Let me explain.=C2=A0
Battery cells are the basic unit of the battery, the place where the chemis=
try that stores and discharges electricity happens. There are a number of l=
arge companies, startups, and research labs working on improving the perfor=
mance of the current most popular chemistry, lithium-ion, and on developing=
 new chemistries altogether.=C2=A0
As a result, the price of battery cells continues to drop. Today=E2=80=99s =
best battery cell technology may be surpassed by something better tomorrow.=
 It=E2=80=99s a competitive, cutthroat market, one that China dominates.=C2=
=A0
Battery cells are largely commoditized. You can buy cells, and essentially =
plug them into the battery pack. As cells get cheaper or more performant, o=
r as new chemistries emerge, you can plug those new cells into new packs.=
=C2=A0
The packs themselves consist of =E2=80=9Cmultiple battery cells, the casing=
, the Battery Management System, cooling system, and connectors.=E2=80=9D T=
he design of the pack influences and is influenced by a number of other pie=
ces in Base=E2=80=99s system. For example, the pack needs to be designed in=
 such a way that it can be installed quickly and easily, since Base has ide=
ntified installation as one of the key bottlenecks, so Base designs them wi=
th =E2=80=9Cconnectors instead of wires that need to be cut and crimped.=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s not the way you=E2=80=99d design a specific battery f=
or optimal performance,=E2=80=9D the team told me, =E2=80=9Cbut how you=E2=
=80=99d design the whole system for optimal performance.=E2=80=9D
That=E2=80=99s the difference to me between deep tech and Vertical Integrat=
ors. A deep tech company designs a product for optimal performance, taking =
on the risk of unproven science or technology to do so, while a Vertical In=
tegrator designs a system for optimal performance, taking a risk on the com=
bination of proven technologies.
A deep tech company may work on a breakthrough cell chemistry; a Vertical I=
ntegrator may integrate that new battery cell once it=E2=80=99s proven.=C2=
=A0
Graphically, I=E2=80=99d break it down like this:
Legacy Industrials, or Incumbents, have vertically integrated supply chains=
 designed around older technologies and processes. Deep Tech companies have=
 unproven technologies that, if they work, could be a very powerful buildin=
g block for many businesses. Vertical Integrators integrate a number of cut=
ting-edge-but-proven technologies; what they need to prove is that they can=
 integrate those technologies into a system that can compete with incumbent=
s.=C2=A0
This may seem like quibbling over semantics, but as more people build, inve=
st in, and compete with startups building in the physical world, understand=
ing the distinctions is important. They offer very different risk profiles =
and potential outcomes.
Properly categorizing Vertical Integrators offers a sharper analytical fram=
ework for this era in tech, just as properly categorizing Aggregators did i=
n the previous one.=C2=A0
Aggregators and Vertical Integrators
Ben Thompson began his canonical Aggregation Theory [ https://substack.com/=
redirect/18320604-8b44-461e-ab3a-b5d84fa43afd?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T=
7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] post in 2015 with an observation, =
=E2=80=9CThe last several articles on Stratechery have formed an unintentio=
nal series.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
The genius of Aggregation Theory was that it tied together seemingly dispar=
ate examples of the most promising tech companies of the era like Uber, Air=
bnb, Netflix, Google, and Facebook into one neat framework.=C2=A0
Thompson realized that the companies he=E2=80=99d been writing about =E2=80=
=93 while offering products ranging from connection with friends to movies =
to rides =E2=80=93 were all representative of a new business model enabled =
by the Internet.=C2=A0
Thanks to the internet, Thompson observed, Aggregators created value not by=
 controlling scarce physical resources, but by aggregating user demand and =
attention, which then attracts suppliers to the platform.=C2=A0
Aggregators, he later wrote, have three characteristics in common:=C2=A0
Direct Relationship with Users
Zero Marginal Cost for Serving Users
Demand-driven Multi-sided Networks with Decreasing Acquisition Costs
Those characteristics allowed them to steal value from the winners of the p=
revious generation:
Previous incumbents, such as newspapers, book publishers, networks, taxi co=
mpanies, and hoteliers, all of whom integrated backwards, lose value in fav=
or of aggregators who aggregate modularized suppliers =E2=80=94 which they =
often don=E2=80=99t pay for =E2=80=94 to consumers/users with whom they hav=
e an exclusive relationship at scale.
Incumbents owned supply, which made them valuable until owning supply becam=
e a liability. Aggregators won by owning demand, and using that demand to g=
et supply to do what they wanted. Think: companies contorting their webpage=
s to be SEO-optimized for Google.=C2=A0
These were among the most successful of the internet businesses. Google and=
 Facebook are worth a combined $3.4 trillion despite the fact that the glob=
al advertising market from with the companies pull most of their revenue wa=
s only worth $370 billion [ https://substack.com/redirect/f8f7d319-ff1c-4f1=
b-ab8f-5b71e57b97dc?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8=
-6m8ExkRNpU ] when Facebook was born in 2004. They, and the rest of the agg=
regators, left a trail of traditional businesses in their wake.=C2=A0
At the end of the 2015 piece, Thompson predicted that Aggregators were comi=
ng for everything:=C2=A0
The Uber and Airbnb examples are especially important: vacant rooms and tax=
is have not been digitized, but they have been disrupted. I suspect that ne=
arly every industry will belatedly discover it has a critical function that=
 can be digitized and commodified, precipitating this shift.
That didn=E2=80=99t end up happening. I tried to explain why I thought it w=
ouldn=E2=80=99t back in 2019, before I wrote Not Boring, in Why There Won=
=E2=80=99t Be Any New Aggregators [ https://substack.com/redirect/ba756fd0-=
f02d-41b6-83ed-50426b27eeb5?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXe=
pKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ], and I think it holds up decently well. The short ver=
sion is that Aggregators had already won the biggest categories that were s=
usceptible to Aggregators, and that the remaining categories were too small=
 to support the necessary model: spend a lot upfront, and make it back on s=
mall margin dollars over a huge amount of transactions.=C2=A0
One addition to that hypothesis is that Aggregators worked for categories t=
hat were either digital =E2=80=93 and therefore where marginal costs for ne=
w supply was practically zero =E2=80=93 or where supply already existed, li=
ke cars or homes. Better allocating those existing resources was a coordina=
tion problem =E2=80=93 an information problem =E2=80=93 and therefore susce=
ptible to Aggregators.=C2=A0
Producing new physical things, however, is a physical problem, and it requi=
res physical solutions.=C2=A0
The companies that succeed in building physical solutions will do so in a w=
ay that=E2=80=99s opposite to Aggregators: by integrating supply.=C2=A0
That is counterintuitive, that this generation of winners in tech will do s=
o by throwing out the lessons of the last generation. But it also makes sen=
se.=20
Aggregators won the Internet by leaning as hard to one side of the business=
 model spectrum as possible; Vertical Integrators will win the physical wor=
ld by leaning as hard to the opposite side as they can.=C2=A0
As with anything in the modern world, you don=E2=80=99t want to be stuck in=
 the middle, attempting to solve physical problems with internet-inspired a=
sset-light solutions.=C2=A0
Or as Roald Dahl put it, via Matilda, =E2=80=9CNever do anything by halves =
if you want to get away with it. Be outrageous. Go the whole hog. Make sure=
 everything you do is so completely crazy it's unbelievable.=E2=80=9D
I=E2=80=99ll admit that Matilda is not an expert on the subject, but Peter =
Thiel is. And almost a decade ago, he made a similar observation: that the =
two ways to make money with new things are either software or =E2=80=9Ccomp=
lex, vertically integrated monopolies.=E2=80=9D
Complex, Vertically Integrated Monopolies
Over the weekend, after I=E2=80=99d already started writing this essay, Ell=
iot [ https://substack.com/redirect/165b3a6c-f241-4925-87fb-6a229f7d454b?j=
=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] sent me=
 his memo on our latest biotech investment. =E2=80=9CA 23-page memo on [red=
acted] for your weekend enjoyment.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
It hit my Slack as Puja and I were headed to Dan and Sienna=E2=80=99s engag=
ement party (congrats lovebirds!), but as soon as we got home, Puja went to=
 bed and I went to my office to read it. Elliot=E2=80=99s memo was an excel=
lent analysis of a Vertical Integrator in biotech, and I can=E2=80=99t wait=
 to tell you about it, but I bring it up here because on page 22, Elliot wr=
ote, =E2=80=9C[Redacted] and [Redacted] have a unique blend of experiences =
and talents that will enable them to execute on building a =E2=80=98complex=
, vertically integrated monopoly,=E2=80=99 as Peter Thiel would frame it.=
=E2=80=9D
I=E2=80=99d never heard Thiel talk about complex, vertically-integrated mon=
opolies, and I was in the middle of writing this piece on complex, vertical=
ly-integrated companies, so I Googled it, and found an old talk that Thiel =
gave at Stanford:=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CIn my mind,=E2=80=9D Thiel says to open the clip, =E2=80=9CThere p=
robably are only two broad categories in the entire history of the last 250=
 years where people have actually come up with new things and made money do=
ing so.=E2=80=9D
One is software. The other:=C2=A0
Is the sort of vertically-integrated, complex monopolies which people did b=
uild in the Second Industrial Revolution at the end of the 19th and start o=
f the 20th century. And so this is like Ford, this is the vertically integr=
ated oil companies like Standard Oil. And what these vertically integrated =
monopolies typically required was this very complex coordination, you=E2=80=
=99ve got a lot of pieces to fit together in just the right way, when you=
=E2=80=99ve assembled it you had a tremendous advantage. This is actually d=
one surprisingly little today, and so I think this is a business form that =
when people can pull it off is very valuable.
I=E2=80=99d had a couple of beers at Dan and Sienna=E2=80=99s party. This w=
as me watching the clip:=C2=A0
My whole thesis [ https://substack.com/redirect/2906dc6f-3eec-400a-98ba-8be=
09dc7570c?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNp=
U ] has been that we=E2=80=99re entering a period that will look more like =
the Second Industrial Revolution than the Internet Era. That the low-hangin=
g software fruit has been picked, but there=E2=80=99s a lot of even juicier=
 fruit in the higher branches if you=E2=80=99re willing to vertically integ=
rate around both software and hardware to get up there.
As Thiel explained, =E2=80=9CIf you look at Tesla or SpaceX, if you ask, yo=
u know, was there a single breakthrough? They certainly innovated on a lot =
of dimensions. I don't think there was a single 10x breakthrough in battery=
 storage or maybe working on some things on rocketry but there was no sort =
of single massive breakthrough. But what was really impressive was integrat=
ing all these pieces together and doing it in a way that was more verticall=
y integrated than most of their competitors.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
It was exactly the distinction I=E2=80=99d made between Deep Tech and Verti=
cal Integrators.=C2=A0
The integration is the innovation.=C2=A0
The clip was timely, first, because I was in the middle of writing this, an=
d second, because on that very same morning, SpaceX gave us as clear a hand=
-off to the Techno-Industrial era as we=E2=80=99re going to get.=20
When NASA determined that Boeing would be unable to retrieve the two US ast=
ronauts it left stranded on the International Space Station, it didn=E2=80=
=99t call in an Aggregator to bring them home. Nor did it call in a thruste=
r startup to go up and replace Starliner=E2=80=99s faulty thrusters.=C2=A0
It called in SpaceX, a Vertical Integrator, which can launch a full rocket =
with a Crew-9 Dragon capsule, dock to the ISS, and return them safely to ea=
rth.
Boeing itself is a prime example of the fact that in physical industries, p=
roviding a thin coordination layer over others=E2=80=99 activities is a los=
ing strategy. In the pursuit of an asset-light business model, the company =
outsourced so much of its supply chain that its planes=E2=80=99 doors routi=
nely fly off and its rockets=E2=80=99 thrusters don=E2=80=99t thrust. Boein=
g=E2=80=99s faulty thrusters, for example, were made by Aerojet Rocketdyne,=
 a division of L3Harris Technologies.
Elon is unique =E2=80=93 he built both SpaceX and Tesla - and Boeing is on =
a particularly embarrassing run, but my suspicion [ https://substack.com/re=
direct/ae546540-361c-4a7c-820d-9091689ea877?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7G=
nA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] is that there are a lot more Boeings =
out there.=C2=A0
Boeing and its ilk will be replaced. Boeing is not the only one; it=E2=80=
=99s just the most visible. For every astronaut stranded in space, there=E2=
=80=99s a grid quietly struggling under its load.
They are old companies with old systems designed around old technology. The=
y can not be rebuilt piece-by-piece like the Ship of Theseus, the systems a=
re too complex. =E2=80=9CMove one thing here, and something else changes th=
ere. Move that and something else shifts.=E2=80=9D
They not be replaced by Aggregators. They will not be fixed with software.=
=20
They will be replaced by Vertical Integrators that rebuild from the ground =
up.
Vertical integration is an old business model whose time has come again, fu=
eled by examples like SpaceX, a funding landscape featuring VCs with billio=
ns to deploy, new technologies with which to build, failed attempts to fix =
just part of the problem, and founders trained and willing to take on the f=
ull thing.=20
Part II
I had planned to send a longer essay with a section on why software alone c=
an=E2=80=99t fix physical problems, case studies on Vertical Integrators li=
ke Base Power Company, Hadrian, Earth AI, Radiant, and Fuse, the advantages=
 and challenges of vertical integration, why Vertical Integrators are VC-ba=
ckable, and even some spicy predictions. I=E2=80=99m not being lazy; those =
sections are mostly written.=C2=A0
But I=E2=80=99m going to keep them safely tucked in another Google Doc for =
a Part II next week, because I want to make sure you leave with one takeawa=
y:=C2=A0
While Vertical Integrators are technology companies, their business models =
should be the opposite of the Aggregator model that won the Internet era. T=
hey should look like the business models that won the Second Industrial Rev=
olution.=C2=A0
Half-measures =E2=80=93 attempts to retain the glory of software margins =
=E2=80=93 are a road to nowhere.=C2=A0
Building new physical things has, and will always have, marginal costs. Tho=
se who embrace that reality and vertically integrate will win.=C2=A0
Next week, we=E2=80=99ll explore some of the companies that are doing it al=
ready.=C2=A0

That=E2=80=99s all for today. We=E2=80=99ll be back in your inbox on Friday=

t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>“Base ass=

 make battery cells… Base believes that cells will be commoditized, a=
nd that </span><strong>pack assembly allows it to benefit from that commodi=
tization and potentially integrate new cell types as better chemistries eme=

5); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>It&#8217=
;s a concrete example of a trend I’ve noticed towards </span><strong>=

/span></p><ol style=3D"margin-top: 0; padding: 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 8px=

=3D"margin: 8px 0 0 32px;"><p style=3D"box-sizing: border-box; color: rgb(5=
4, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0=
; padding-left: 4px;">Offer products that are better, faster, or cheaper (o=
ften all three).</p></li></ol><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size=
: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">For Vertical Integrators, t=
he integration is the innovation. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 5=
5); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Let’s go=
 back to the Base example to unpack that. Battery pack assembly is one of t=

ckquote style=3D"border-left: 4px solid #0c2427; margin: 20px 0; padding: 0=
;"><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; =
margin: 0 0 20px 0; margin-left: 20px;"><em><span>To deploy batteries and f=
ix the grid, Base can’t just build a battery; it needs to build a ver=
tically integrated power </span><strong>company</strong><span> around batte=
ries. </span></em></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 1=
6px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0; margin-left: 20px;"><em>The pow=
er company needs to excel at a whole lot of things: manufacturing, finance,=
 distributed systems, power trading, customer acquisition, consumer softwar=
e, regulatory, and installation, to start. It needs to become a Retail Elec=
tric Provider (REP) itself.</em></p></blockquote><p style=3D"color: rgb(54,=
 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>Ea=
ch of those things is hard but achievable. What’s novel is their comb=

ermutation comes with trade-offs: “Move one thing here, and something=

em>else </em><span>shifts.” </span></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54=
, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">The dec=
ision to manufacture packs but not cells is one of many such decisions, and=

55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Battery c=
ells are the basic unit of the battery, the place where the chemistry that =
stores and discharges electricity happens. There are a number of large comp=
anies, startups, and research labs working on improving the performance of =
the current most popular chemistry, lithium-ion, and on developing new chem=
istries altogether. </p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static=

ntinues to drop. Today’s best battery cell technology may be surpasse=
d by something better tomorrow. It’s a competitive, cutthroat market,=
 one that China dominates. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); fon=
t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Battery cells are lar=
gely commoditized. You can buy cells, and essentially plug them into the ba=
ttery pack. As cells get cheaper or more performant, or as new chemistries =
emerge, you can plug those new cells into new packs. </p><p style=3D"c=
olor: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px=
 0;">The packs themselves consist of “multiple battery cells, the cas=
ing, the Battery Management System, cooling system, and connectors.” =
The design of the pack influences and is influenced by a number of other pi=
eces in Base’s system. For example, the pack needs to be designed in =
such a way that it can be installed quickly and easily, since Base has iden=
tified installation as one of the key bottlenecks, so Base designs them wit=
h “connectors instead of wires that need to be cut and crimped.&#8221=
; </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height=
: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>“It’s not the way you’=
d design a specific </span><em>battery</em><span> for optimal performance,&=
#8221; the team told me, “but how you’d design the whole </span=
><em>system</em><span> for optimal performance.”</span></p><p style=
=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0=
 20px 0;"><span>That’s the difference to me between deep tech and Ver=
tical Integrators. </span><strong>A deep tech company designs a </strong><e=
m><strong>product</strong></em><strong> for optimal performance, taking on =
the risk of unproven science or technology to do so, while a Vertical Integ=
rator designs a </strong><em><strong>system</strong></em><strong> for optim=
al performance, taking a risk on the combination of proven technologies.</s=
trong></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height:=
 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">A deep tech company may work on a breakthrough =
cell chemistry; a Vertical Integrator may integrate that new battery cell o=
nce it’s proven. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-si=
ze: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Graphically, I’d br=
eak it down like this:</p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static" s=

mpanies have unproven technologies that, if they work, could be a very powe=
rful building block for many businesses. </span><strong>Vertical Integrator=
s</strong><span> integrate a number of cutting-edge-but-proven technologies=
; what they need to prove is that they can integrate those technologies int=
o a system that can compete with incumbents. </span></p><p style=3D"co=
lor: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px =
0;">This may seem like quibbling over semantics, but as more people build, =
invest in, and compete with startups building in the physical world, unders=
tanding the distinctions is important. They offer very different risk profi=
les and potential outcomes.</p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-siz=
e: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Properly categorizing Vert=
ical Integrators offers a sharper analytical framework for this era in tech=
, just as properly categorizing Aggregators did in the previous one. <=
/p><h3 class=3D"header-anchor-post" style=3D"-moz-appearance: optimizelegib=
ility; -moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased; -webkit-appearance: optimizele=
gibility; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; appearance: optimizelegibili=
ty; color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-family: 'SF Pro Display', -apple-system-he=

UI Symbol'; font-size: 1.375em; font-weight: bold; line-height: 1.16em; mar=
gin: 1em 0 0.625em 0; position: relative;"><strong>Aggregators and Vertical=
 Integrators</strong></h3><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16=
px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>Ben Thompson began his ca=
nonical </span><strong><a href=3D"https://substack.com/redirect/18320604-8b=
44-461e-ab3a-b5d84fa43afd?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepK=
bqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU" rel style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); text-decoration: un=
derline;">Aggregation Theory</a></strong><span> post in 2015 with an observ=
ation, “The last several articles on Stratechery have formed an unint=
entional series.” </span></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55);=
 font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">The genius of Agg=
regation Theory was that it tied together seemingly disparate examples of t=
he most promising tech companies of the era like Uber, Airbnb, Netflix, Goo=
gle, and Facebook into one neat framework. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(=
54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Thomp=
son realized that the companies he’d been writing about – while=
 offering products ranging from connection with friends to movies to rides =
– were all representative of a new business model enabled by the Inte=
rnet. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-he=
ight: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Thanks to the internet, Thompson observed,=
 Aggregators created value not by controlling scarce physical resources, bu=
t by aggregating user demand and attention, which then attracts suppliers t=
o the platform. </p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static" st=

<li style=3D"margin: 8px 0 0 32px;"><p style=3D"box-sizing: border-box; col=
or: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0; margin-=

><li style=3D"margin: 8px 0 0 32px;"><p style=3D"box-sizing: border-box; co=
lor: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0; margin=
-bottom: 0; padding-left: 4px;">Demand-driven Multi-sided Networks with Dec=
reasing Acquisition Costs</p></li></ol><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); =
font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Those characterist=
ics allowed them to steal value from the winners of the previous generation=
:</p><blockquote style=3D"border-left: 4px solid #0c2427; margin: 20px 0; p=
adding: 0;"><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-heigh=
t: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0; margin-left: 20px;"><em>Previous incumbents, s=
uch as newspapers, book publishers, networks, taxi companies, and hoteliers=
, all of whom integrated backwards, lose value in favor of aggregators who =
aggregate modularized suppliers — which they often don’t pay fo=
r — to consumers/users with whom they have an exclusive relationship =
at scale.</em></p></blockquote><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-siz=
e: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>Incumbents owned sup=
ply, which made them valuable until owning supply became a liability. Aggre=
gators won by owning </span><strong>demand</strong><span>, and using that d=
emand to get supply to do what they wanted. Think: companies contorting the=
ir webpages to be SEO-optimized for Google. </span></p><p style=3D"col=
or: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0=
;"><span>These were among the most successful of the internet businesses. G=
oogle and Facebook are worth a combined $3.4 trillion despite the fact that=
 the global advertising market from with the companies pull most of their r=
evenue was only worth </span><a href=3D"https://substack.com/redirect/f8f7d=
319-ff1c-4f1b-ab8f-5b71e57b97dc?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjm=
lXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU" rel style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); text-decorati=
on: underline;">$370 billion</a><span> when Facebook was born in 2004. They=
, and the rest of the aggregators, left a trail of traditional businesses i=
n their wake. </span></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size=
: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">At the end of the 2015 piec=
e, Thompson predicted that Aggregators were coming for everything: </p=
><blockquote style=3D"border-left: 4px solid #0c2427; margin: 20px 0; paddi=
ng: 0;"><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 2=
6px; margin: 0 0 20px 0; margin-left: 20px;"><em><span>The Uber and Airbnb =
examples are especially important: vacant rooms and taxis have not been dig=
itized, but they have been disrupted. </span><strong>I suspect that nearly =
every industry will belatedly discover it has a critical function that can =
be digitized and commodified</strong><span>, precipitating this shift.</spa=
n></em></p></blockquote><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px=
; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>That didn’t end up ha=
ppening. I tried to explain why I thought it wouldn’t back in 2019, b=
efore I wrote Not Boring, in </span><em><a href=3D"https://substack.com/red=
irect/ba756fd0-f02d-41b6-83ed-50426b27eeb5?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7Gn=
A_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU" rel style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); te=
xt-decoration: underline;">Why There Won’t Be Any New Aggregators</a>=
</em><span>, and I think it holds up decently well. The short version is th=
at Aggregators had already won the biggest categories that were susceptible=
 to Aggregators, and that the remaining categories were too small to suppor=
t the necessary model: spend a lot upfront, and make it back on small margi=
n dollars over a huge amount of transactions. </span></p><p style=3D"c=
olor: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px=
 0;">One addition to that hypothesis is that Aggregators worked for categor=
ies that were either digital – and therefore where marginal costs for=
 new supply was practically zero – or where supply already existed, l=
ike cars or homes. Better allocating those existing resources was a coordin=
ation problem – an information problem – and therefore suscepti=
ble to Aggregators. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size:=
 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><strong>Producing new physic=
al things, however, is a physical problem, and it requires physical solutio=
ns. </strong></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; =
line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">The companies that succeed in build=
ing physical solutions will do so in a way that’s opposite to Aggrega=
tors: by integrating supply. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); f=
ont-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">That is counterintu=
itive, that this generation of winners in tech will do so by throwing out t=
he lessons of the last generation. But it also makes sense. </p><p style=3D=
"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20=
px 0;"><strong>Aggregators won the Internet by leaning as hard to one side =
of the business model spectrum as possible; Vertical Integrators will win t=
he physical world by leaning as hard to the opposite side as they can.</str=
ong><span> </span></p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static" =

;</p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px=
; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Or as Roald Dahl put it, via Matilda, “Never d=

 whole hog. Make sure everything you do is so completely crazy it's unbelie=

height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>I’ll admit that Matilda is n=
ot an expert on the subject, but Peter Thiel is. And almost a decade ago, h=
e made a similar observation: </span><strong>that the two ways to make mone=
y with new things are either software or “complex, vertically integra=
ted monopolies.”</strong></p><h3 class=3D"header-anchor-post" style=
=3D"-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility; -moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialias=

ht: bold; line-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0; position: relative;=
"><strong>Complex, Vertically Integrated Monopolies</strong></h3><p style=
=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0=
 20px 0;"><span>Over the weekend, after I’d already started writing t=
his essay, </span><a href=3D"https://substack.com/redirect/165b3a6c-f241-49=
25-87fb-6a229f7d454b?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa=
8-6m8ExkRNpU" rel style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); text-decoration: underli=
ne;">Elliot</a><span> sent me his memo on our latest biotech investment. &#=
8220;A 23-page memo on [redacted] for your weekend enjoyment.” <=
/span></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height:=
 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>It hit my Slack as Puja and I were headed=
 to Dan and Sienna’s engagement party (congrats lovebirds!), but as s=
oon as we got home, Puja went to bed and I went to my office to read it. El=
liot’s memo was an excellent analysis of a Vertical Integrator in bio=
tech, and I can’t wait to tell you about it, but I bring it up here b=

r: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;=

t: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>As Thiel explained, “If you look =

; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><strong>The integration is the in=

16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">The clip was timely, first, b=
ecause I was in the middle of writing this, and second, because on that ver=

ont-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">When NASA determine=
d that Boeing would be unable to retrieve the two US astronauts it left str=
anded on the International Space Station, it didn’t call in an Aggreg=
ator to bring them home. Nor did it call in a thruster startup to go up and=

b(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">It =
called in SpaceX, a Vertical Integrator, which can launch a full rocket wit=
h a Crew-9 Dragon capsule, dock to the ISS, and return them safely to earth=
=2E</p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55=
); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px=
; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Boeing itself is a prime example of the fact that in=

t thrust. Boeing’s faulty thrusters, for example, were made by Aeroje=
t Rocketdyne, a division of L3Harris Technologies.</p><div class=3D"caption=

p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; marg=
in: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>They are old companies with old systems designed aro=
und old technology. They can not be rebuilt piece-by-piece like the Ship of=
 Theseus, the systems are too complex. “Move one thing here, and some=
thing else changes there. Move </span><em>that</em><span> and something </s=
pan><em>else </em><span>shifts.”</span></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54,=
 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">They not=
 be replaced by Aggregators. They will not be fixed with software. </p><p s=
tyle=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin:=
 0 0 20px 0;"><strong>They will be replaced by Vertical Integrators that re=
build from the ground up.</strong></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); f=
ont-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Vertical integratio=
n is an old business model whose time has come again, fueled by examples li=
ke SpaceX, a funding landscape featuring VCs with billions to deploy, new t=
echnologies with which to build, failed attempts to fix just part of the pr=
oblem, and founders trained and willing to take on the full thing. </p><h3 =

=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0=
 20px 0;">I had planned to send a longer essay with a section on why softwa=
re alone can’t fix physical problems, case studies on Vertical Integr=
ators like Base Power Company, Hadrian, Earth AI, Radiant, and Fuse, the ad=
vantages and challenges of vertical integration, why Vertical Integrators a=
re VC-backable, and even some spicy predictions. I’m not being lazy; =
those sections are mostly written. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, =
55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">But I’m=
 going to keep them safely tucked in another Google Doc for a Part II next =
week, because I want to make sure you leave with one takeaway: </p><p =
style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin=
: 0 0 20px 0;"><strong>While Vertical Integrators are technology companies,=
 their business models should be the opposite of the Aggregator model that =
won the Internet era. They should look like the business models that won th=
e Second Industrial Revolution. </strong></p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54=
, 55, 55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Half-me=
asures – attempts to retain the glory of software margins – are=
 a road to nowhere. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, 55); font-size:=
 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Building new physical things=
 has, and will always have, marginal costs. Those who embrace that reality =
and vertically integrate will win. </p><p style=3D"color: rgb(54, 55, =
55); font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Next week, we=
’ll explore some of the companies that are doing it already. </p=
